name: Cistercian Route: Santes Creus and Figuerola del Camp Loop
name_original: Mazinger Z, Santes Creus, Figuerola del Camp – ruta circular
description: Cycle a moderate 21.2-mile circular route past the Mazinger Z Statue and the Royal Monastery of Santes Creus in Catalonia.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Cabra Del Camp traverse a landscape characterized by rolling hills and agricultural plains, offering varied terrain for cyclists. The region features a mix of open countryside and historical sites, with routes often connecting charming villages and cultural landmarks. Elevation changes are present, ranging from moderate climbs to more challenging ascents, particularly on routes leading to viewpoints. The area provides a diverse backdrop for cycling, from scenic rural paths to routes exploring local heritage. Best touring cycling routes around Cabra Del Camp The most popular touring cycling route is Cistercian Route: Santes Creus and Figuerola del Camp Loop, a 21.2 miles (34.2 km) trail that takes 2 hours 36 minutes to complete. This moderate route connects historical Cistercian sites and rural landscapes. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is View of Barberà de la Conca – Font d’en Guàrdies loop from Cabra del Camp, a difficult 24.7 miles (39.8 km) path. This route features significant elevation gain, offering panoramic views of the surrounding area. Local touring cyclists also love the Old Bridge of Montblanc – Santa Maria Church (Montblanc) loop from Barberà de la Conca, a 53.2 miles (85.7 km) trail leading through historical towns and cultural landmarks, often completed in about 7 hours 35 minutes. Touring cycling around Cabra Del Camp is defined by rolling agricultural lands, historical sites, and scenic viewpoints. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Cabra Del Camp are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 20 reviews. More than 500 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Cabra Del Camp's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Cabra Del Camp?
A: There are over 80 touring cycling routes around Cabra Del Camp, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels and preferences. These routes traverse a landscape of rolling hills and agricultural plains, connecting charming villages and historical sites.

Q: Are there easy touring cycling routes in Cabra Del Camp suitable for beginners?
A: Yes, Cabra Del Camp offers some easy touring cycling routes. While the majority are moderate to difficult, there is at least one easy route available. These routes typically feature gentler terrain, making them suitable for those new to touring cycling or looking for a relaxed ride.

Q: What are some challenging touring cycling routes for experienced cyclists in Cabra Del Camp?
A: Experienced cyclists looking for a challenge will find plenty of difficult routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19837663" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Bridge of Montblanc – Santa Maria Church (Montblanc) loop from Barberà de la Conca&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 53.2-mile (85.7 km) trail with significant elevation changes, leading through historical towns. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17527238" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mazinger Z Statue – Coll de la Torreta loop from el Pla de Santa Maria&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 77.1 miles (77.1 km) and also presents a considerable challenge.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes in Cabra Del Camp?
A: Many of the touring cycling routes in Cabra Del Camp are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10421360" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santes Creus – Mazinger Z Statue loop from el Pla de Santa Maria&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 32-mile (51.5 km) route that offers a varied cycling experience.

Q: What kind of attractions can I see along the touring cycling routes in Cabra Del Camp?
A: The routes often pass by historical sites and natural viewpoints. You can explore various castles, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/745758" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Prenafeta Castle&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2716133" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saburella Castle&lt;/a&gt;. For scenic views, consider routes that lead to summits like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3540617" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Cogulla de Figuerola del Camp Summit and Miramar Cross&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4127541" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roc del Cogulló Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: When is the best time of year for touring cycling in Cabra Del Camp?
A: The spring and autumn months generally offer the most pleasant conditions for touring cycling in Cabra Del Camp. Temperatures are milder, and the landscape is vibrant. Summer can be quite warm, especially in the midday sun, while winter can bring cooler temperatures, though cycling is often still possible on clear days.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Cabra Del Camp?
A: The touring cycling routes in Cabra Del Camp are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars. Reviewers often praise the diverse backdrop, from scenic rural paths to routes exploring local heritage, and the mix of open countryside and historical sites.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in Cabra Del Camp?
A: While specific 'family-friendly' designations are not always explicit, routes with an 'easy' difficulty grade and shorter distances would be most suitable. These routes typically avoid steep climbs and heavy traffic, providing a safer and more enjoyable experience for families. Always check the route details for elevation and terrain before heading out.

Q: Is parking available near the touring cycling routes in Cabra Del Camp?
A: In rural areas like Cabra Del Camp, parking is generally available in or near the starting points of many routes, particularly in villages or designated recreational areas. It's advisable to check local maps or route descriptions for specific parking recommendations before your trip.

Q: Can I access touring cycling routes in Cabra Del Camp using public transport?
A: Accessing routes by public transport can vary. While some larger towns in the region may have train or bus connections, direct access to the starting points of all touring cycling routes might be limited. Researching local bus services or regional train lines that allow bicycles is recommended for specific routes.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or food along the touring cycling routes?
A: Many touring cycling routes in Cabra Del Camp connect or pass through charming villages and towns. These often provide opportunities to stop for refreshments, find cafes, or purchase supplies. Planning your route with potential stops in mind will enhance your cycling experience.
tips:
The gigantic robot has been there since 1979, thanks to a horny real estate developer who wanted to create a playground as a claim to sell the urbanization plots. The park did not prosper (urbanization, yes), but Mazinger remains there.
http://omnibus-dubitandum.blogspot.com/2013/06/el-mazinger-z-de-tarragona.html

The Monastery of Santa María de Santes Creus is a Cistercian abbey built in the 12th century. The grandeur of the monastery is due to the donations received from the nobility who wanted to be buried here.
Take advantage of the sunset to enjoy the light and the birds of this curious place. But any time of day can be spectacular due to the large number of birds that inhabit these wetlands.

Watchtower and modern defense, with a circular floor of 6.5 m in outer diameter and about 10 m high, from the base to the crown. The work is made of masonry, with walls 0.80 m thick, kept the same on all floors. The access to the tower (115 cm wide and 180 cm high), located on the ground floor, faces east and has a stone lintel and chopped stone jambs. In addition, several loopholes and three square windows are also observed at the top. The structure is topped by a set of three gargoyles for drainage and, at the top, three corbels, which must have supported three battlements, currently being rebuilt. Inside the tower, a basement has been preserved covered with a stone vault tied with lime mortar, which is accessed through a small rectangular opening of about 60 x 70 cm. located on the outside floor, and which had the function of a cistern. There is nothing left of the three ceilings that were there: those on the ground floor and the two upper floors. On the wall of this basement there is a written inscription from 1615. The technique used in construction is ordinary masonry, plastered on the outside, with a very uniform appearance throughout the volume.

This little town is on a hill, at the foot of the Sierra Carbonària. A place steeped in history that retains its rural charm. It is worth a visit to its historic center through the Literary Walk route, based on poems by Josep Colet i Giralt and which allows you to discover twenty-two places in the municipality.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: The route is designed for touring bicycles and generally takes around 2 hours and 36 minutes to complete. This duration is an estimate and can vary based on your cycling pace, stops for sightseeing, and fitness level.

faqs.question: Is this touring bicycle route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as moderate in difficulty. While it's not overly strenuous, it does involve some elevation changes. Touring cyclists with a reasonable level of fitness and some experience should find it enjoyable. Sections of the wider Cistercian Route can be challenging, but this specific touring bicycle route likely sticks to more manageable paved roads and well-maintained gravel paths.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: You can expect a varied terrain, primarily consisting of paved roads and well-maintained gravel paths suitable for touring bicycles. The area is known for good asphalt and scenic, curvy roads. You'll cycle through agricultural fields, almond trees, olive groves, and vineyards, with some ancient paths and dry stone walls, characteristic of the Alt Camp region.

faqs.question: What are the main sights and landmarks along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers a rich blend of cultural, historical, and unique sights. Key highlights include the iconic Mazinger Z Statue, the historic Royal Monastery of Santes Creus, the Aiguamolls del Pla Wetlands, the ancient Torre de la Mixarda, and the charming Church of Sant Jaume, Figuerola del Camp.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several other well-known paths. You'll find yourself on parts of the Camí de Sant Jaume de Tarragona a Lleida - Etapa 3, GR-175 BTT, and the historic Ruta del Císter. It also intersects with El Camí IT6TR5 El Camí Central: de Castelló de la Plana a Montserrat, among others.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region generally offers pleasant cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn are often ideal, with milder temperatures and beautiful scenery as the fields and vineyards change. Summers can be hot, so starting early in the morning is advisable if cycling during peak summer months.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for this exact route aren't detailed, Santes Creus, being a significant historical site and part of the Ruta del Císter, typically has designated parking facilities for visitors. Figuerola del Camp, as a small town, also offers parking options. It's recommended to check local parking availability near your chosen starting point in either Santes Creus or Figuerola del Camp.

faqs.question: Is this touring bicycle route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally welcome in many outdoor areas, this is a touring bicycle route that often uses public roads and shared paths. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure it is well-behaved, accustomed to cycling alongside a bike, and kept on a leash, especially in populated areas or near traffic. Always carry water for your dog and be mindful of local regulations regarding pets in historical sites like the Royal Monastery of Santes Creus.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No specific permits are required to cycle this route itself, as it uses public roads and established paths. However, if you plan to enter certain attractions like the Royal Monastery of Santes Creus, there may be entrance fees. It's advisable to check the official websites of any specific sites you wish to visit for their current admission policies and opening hours.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or find refreshments along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, both Santes Creus and Figuerola del Camp are towns where you can find cafes, restaurants, and other amenities. The wider Ruta del Císter, which this route connects to, is known for having infrastructure that supports cyclists, including places for refreshments. You'll have opportunities to refuel and enjoy local cuisine.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a touring bicycle trip on this route?
faqs.answer: For this touring bicycle route, essential items include a well-maintained touring bicycle, a helmet, appropriate cycling attire, water bottles, snacks, a basic repair kit (spare tube, pump, tire levers), and navigation tools (like a Komoot device or app). Sunscreen, sunglasses, and a camera to capture the diverse scenery and unique landmarks are also recommended.
